story:
In a reeking cave whose mouth looked out on wilderness, the court sorcerer found his way barred by a hulking beast. Far off, his falconers called his name. The creature’s silhouette filled the exit. “A flag bearer is the one I want!” it snarled. The sorcerer, keeping his voice level, weighed the demand. Would the beast release him if he sought such a prisoner? The beast swore he wanted the king to know of him at last, to pay for overlooking him. He raged about flags and fame, about being seen from the castle walls.

The sorcerer sat, back to cold stone, and asked the single word that mattered: “Why?” The beast roared for a flag bearer again, as if the title itself were a trophy. The sorcerer tried another approach. He offered a bargain: he would ask the king for a flag bearer to appease the beast. If he failed, he would return and give himself up to spare his men the shame of finding him dead in this rank hole. Only, he asked, if it came to that, that the beast kill him quickly. His voice thinned with disgust. The stench, he said, could not be borne.

The shuffling stopped. Silence trembled between them. The sorcerer pressed his advantage. “Is this the cave of Daletop?” he asked, as if he half-hoped the answer might redeem these depths. “Can you look down over the king’s fields and Bower Castle? Feel the high wind? No. This place is a gangrenous stump at the end of the earth.”

He spun a new picture. Daletop, high and clean, with banners catching the sun. Caverns beneath it that wound for miles. Secret waterways that laced beneath the castle itself. From Daletop, he said, he could travel those paths in hours and deliver the beast’s request. From Daletop, in times of war, even the king’s people would thread those caverns to safety—so the creature would never be overlooked again. From that high mouth, the beast could see the king’s standards and be seen in turn. From that strong perch, there would be enemies enough to stain the largest flag red.

The beast’s rage cracked. He toppled to his side and wept. The sorcerer rose and, daring, cradled the creature’s head in both hands, his whisper warm and steady. Tomorrow at dusk, he said, he would send a falcon to this mouth. Follow the bird half the night and it would lead him to Daletop. That cave was worthy of a noble beast, not this sore on the earth. There the air was bright. There the world could finally witness him. There, the sorcerer implied, a tribute could be had without squalor, and the king would at last be forced to take note.

“Go,” howled the beast. “Go now!” He wailed like a storm wind in a hollow. The sorcerer held him a moment longer, then stepped back, cloak snapping as he left the foul air behind. Outside, the wilderness waited. The falconers still called his name. He had traded fear for promise, squalor for sky, and a monster’s hunger for a vision bright enough to move it. Tomorrow, a falcon would rise, and the beast would climb toward a higher mouth and a cleaner wind.
